content:
2 min read
Just now

--

I have a question.
What if we’ve been asking the wrong questions all along?
For years we’ve debated presidents, political parties, Congress, inflation, immigration, interest rates, Wall Street, foreign countries, and every election cycle. Every four years we convince ourselves that the next election will solve our problems.
But what if America’s greatest challenge isn’t sitting in the White House?
What if it’s sitting in our own neighborhoods?
Over the last two decades, I’ve watched our country go through September 11, the housing boom, the financial crisis, COVID, and now another period of economic uncertainty.
Through every one of those moments, I’ve noticed something that concerns me even more than politics.
Little by little, we’ve stopped investing in one another.
We’ve stopped knowing our neighbors.
We’ve stopped supporting local businesses the way we once did.
We’ve become more connected online and, somehow, more disconnected where it matters most.
A nation isn’t built from the top down.
It’s built from the ground up.
Strong families build strong neighborhoods.
Strong neighborhoods build strong communities.
Strong communities build strong cities.
Strong cities build strong states.
Strong states build a strong America.
That’s the foundation.
Government was never meant to replace that foundation. It was created to serve it.
Politicians are public servants, not celebrities.
Their job is to listen to the people – not to become influencers whose next interview, rally, or social media post receives more attention than the communities they were elected to represent.
Somewhere along the way, we’ve reversed the order.
We started believing that politics creates society.
History shows the opposite.
Society creates politics.
Government is a reflection of its people. When citizens become more involved, more responsible, more united, and more engaged, government eventually follows.
No president – whether it’s Donald Trump or anyone else – can rebuild a neighborhood where neighbors no longer know each other.
No Congress can force people to support local businesses.
No governor can create trust between families.
No law can replace personal responsibility.
Those things begin with us.
Instead of asking only:
“What is Trump going to do?”
“What is the next president going to do?”
Maybe we should also ask ourselves:
What am I doing to strengthen my community?
How can I support the businesses that employ my neighbors?
How can I mentor a young person?
How can I make my street, my neighborhood, and my city a better place?
How can I contribute instead of simply criticizing?
Politics has its place.
Government has its role.
But neither should ever become the center of American life.
The American people should.
Because in the end, governments don’t build great nations.
People do.
America has always been at its best when citizens led with responsibility, character, and purpose – and government simply followed their lead.
Maybe it’s time we remembered that.
Because before we’re Republicans or Democrats…
Before we’re conservatives or liberals…
Before we’re MAGA or anti-MAGA…
We’re neighbors.
We’re communities.
We’re Americans.
And if we rebuild that foundation together, the politics will eventually take care of itself.
